title: SQL Joins and Aggregation
summary: Combine data from multiple tables using JOIN, and summarise data using GROUP BY, HAVING, and aggregate functions.
content_html:
Why Joins?

Real applications store data in multiple normalised tables. A JOIN combines rows from two or more tables based on a related column (usually a FK-PK relationship), allowing you to retrieve data that spans multiple tables in a single query.

INNER JOIN

Returns only rows where the join condition matches in both tables (rows with no match are excluded).

-- Get movie title WITH its rating name:
SELECT m.title, r.ratingName
FROM   Movie m
INNER JOIN Rating r ON m.ratingCode = r.ratingCode;

-- Result:
title                     | ratingName
-----------------------------------------
The Hunger Games          | Parental Guidance
Silver Linings Playbook   | Mature Accompanied (15+)
The Hangover              | Mature Accompanied (15+)

LEFT JOIN (LEFT OUTER JOIN)

Returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table. If there is no match on the right, NULL is returned for right table columns.

-- Find movies with NO cast members yet:
SELECT m.title, c.actorNo
FROM   Movie m
LEFT JOIN Casting c ON m.movieNo = c.movieNo
WHERE c.actorNo IS NULL;

Multi-Table JOIN

-- Get actor names and movie titles for all castings:
SELECT a.firstName, a.surname, m.title
FROM   Casting c
INNER JOIN Actor  a ON c.actorNo = a.actorNo
INNER JOIN Movie  m ON c.movieNo = m.movieNo
ORDER BY a.surname, m.title;

GROUP BY with Aggregates

-- Count movies per rating code:
SELECT ratingCode, COUNT(*) AS Total
FROM   Movie
GROUP BY ratingCode
ORDER BY Total DESC;

-- Average runtime per year (only years with avg > 100):
SELECT relyear,
       COUNT(*)          AS MovieCount,
       ROUND(AVG(runtime), 1) AS AvgRuntime
FROM   Movie
GROUP BY relyear
HAVING AVG(runtime) > 100
ORDER BY relyear;

Subqueries

-- Find movies with runtime above the average:
SELECT title, runtime
FROM   Movie
WHERE  runtime > (SELECT AVG(runtime) FROM Movie);

-- Find actors who have appeared in more than 2 movies:
SELECT a.firstName, a.surname, COUNT(*) AS MovieCount
FROM   Actor a
INNER JOIN Casting c ON a.actorNo = c.actorNo
GROUP BY a.actorNo, a.firstName, a.surname
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;

SQL Execution Order

1. FROM    - identify tables
2. JOIN    - combine tables
3. WHERE   - filter individual rows
4. GROUP BY - group rows
5. HAVING  - filter groups
6. SELECT  - compute output columns
7. ORDER BY - sort output

exercises.title: INNER JOIN
exercises.prompt: An INNER JOIN between Movie and Rating returns:
options: Only rows where ratingCode matches in both tables
All rows from Movie plus NULLs for Rating
All rows from both tables combined
Only rows from the Rating table
exercises.answer: Only rows where ratingCode matches in both tables

exercises.title: LEFT JOIN
options: All rows from the left table; NULLs for unmatched right table columns
Only matching rows from both tables
All rows from the right table; NULLs for unmatched left rows
The union of both tables
exercises.answer: All rows from the left table; NULLs for unmatched right table columns

exercises.title: JOIN condition
exercises.prompt: In a JOIN between Movie and Rating, the ON condition should be:
options: Movie.ratingCode = Rating.ratingCode
Movie.movieNo = Rating.ratingCode
Movie.title = Rating.ratingName
Movie.relyear = Rating.ratingCode

exercises.title: GROUP BY result
exercises.prompt: SELECT ratingCode, COUNT(*) FROM Movie GROUP BY ratingCode — what does this query return?
options: One row per ratingCode with the count of movies in that group
All movies sorted by ratingCode
The total number of movies in the entire table
One row per movie with its ratingCode
exercises.answer: One row per ratingCode with the count of movies in that group

exercises.title: HAVING vs WHERE
options: Groups where the aggregate count is greater than 2
Individual rows where a column value exceeds 2
Tables that have more than 2 columns
Results where two conditions are met
exercises.answer: Groups where the aggregate count is greater than 2

exercises.title: Subquery
exercises.prompt: A subquery in the WHERE clause is used to:
options: Compare a value against the result of another SELECT statement
Create a new table from existing data
Define a join condition between two tables
Sort the final result set
exercises.answer: Compare a value against the result of another SELECT statement

exercises.title: SQL execution order
exercises.prompt: In SQL, which clause is evaluated BEFORE GROUP BY?
options: WHERE
HAVING
SELECT
ORDER BY

exercises.title: Multi-table join
exercises.prompt: To retrieve actor names and movie titles via the Casting table, you need:
options: Two INNER JOINs: Casting-Actor and Casting-Movie
One JOIN between Actor and Movie directly
A Cartesian product of all three tables
A subquery for each table
exercises.answer: Two INNER JOINs: Casting-Actor and Casting-Movie
